<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Forcing and independence</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

← Notebook
1 Forcing posets 2 Generic filters 3 Truth lemma 4 Cohen reals 5 ¬CH 6 Applications

Forcing and independence

Cohen's 1963 technique for building a model of ZFC where you decide what is true. Pile up partial conditions until a generic filter exists; the conditions force statements into the extension; choose the poset to make the continuum hypothesis fail. The same machine settles Suslin, Whitehead, and a long list of other questions ZFC alone refuses to answer.

Gödel had shown in 1940 that CH is consistent with ZFC, by building the constructible universe $L$. The other direction — is $\neg$CH also consistent? — sat open for two decades. Cohen's forcing answered yes, and along the way invented the most flexible tool in modern set theory: a way to construct, from a model $M$ of ZFC, a larger model $M[G]$ in which a designed list of new sets exists.

The plot of this page: assemble a poset $\mathbb P$ of "partial information" called conditions; identify $\mathbb P$-generic filters $G$ over a countable model $M$; show that the extension $M[G]$ is again a model of ZFC and that statements about $G$ are decided by a syntactically defined forcing relation $p\Vdash\varphi$. Pick $\mathbb P=2^{<\omega}$ to add a new real, or its $\aleph_2$-fold product to add $\aleph_2$ reals at once and make CH fail. Then survey other independence theorems and Martin's axiom.

1. Forcing posets and dense subsets

A forcing notion is a partially ordered set $(\mathbb P,\le)$ with a maximum element $\mathbb 1\in\mathbb P$. Read $p\le q$ as "$p$ is stronger than $q$" — $p$ commits to more about the unknown object you are building.

Sign conventions vary. Cohen and Kunen use $p\le q$ for "$p$ is stronger" (going down the order pins more bits). Some authors flip the order. We use Cohen/Kunen here. The maximum $\mathbb 1$ is the empty commitment — "I have decided nothing yet".

The canonical example: Cohen forcing $\mathbb P=2^{<\omega}$, the set of finite binary strings, ordered by extension. The empty string $\langle\rangle=\mathbb 1$ is on top; $\langle 0\rangle$ and $\langle 1\rangle$ are stronger; each condition has exactly two immediate extensions. The poset is the infinite binary tree growing downward.

A subset $D\subseteq\mathbb P$ is dense iff for every $p\in\mathbb P$ there exists $q\in D$ with $q\le p$. Density says: every condition can be extended into $D$. Each dense set is a question we want answered, and meeting it is an answer.

Dense subsets in the Cohen poset $2^{<\omega}$
click a "question" to highlight a dense set

Two conditions $p,q$ are compatible if some $r\le p,q$ exists; otherwise incompatible (notation $p\perp q$). In Cohen forcing, two strings are compatible iff one extends the other. An antichain is a set of pairwise incompatible conditions; the countable chain condition (ccc) says every antichain is countable. Cohen forcing satisfies ccc — it preserves all cardinals, a key technical fact for the $\neg$CH application below.

See also
  • ZFC axioms

Used in
Forcing and independence · Generic filters and the model $M[G]$

(function(){

    [{s:'000'},{s:'001'},{s:'010'},{s:'011'},{s:'100'},{s:'101'},{s:'110'},{s:'111'}],
  ];
  function inDense(s, q){
    if(q==='bit2-equals-1') return s.length>=2 && s[1]==='1';
    if(q==='length-ge-3')   return s.length>=3;
    if(q==='ends-in-01')    return s.length>=2 && s.endsWith('01');
    if(q==='differs-from-zero') return s.length>=1 && s.indexOf('1')>=0;

    if(q==='ends-in-01')    return "D = {p : p ends in 01}.\nDense? Yes — given any p, the extension p·01 is in D and below p.\nThis is the kind of density that pins specific finite patterns into r_G.";
    if(q==='differs-from-zero') return "D_s = {p : p ≠ s↾|p|} for s = 000… (the all-zeros real).\nDense? Yes — given p, extend by the bit opposite to s at the next position.\nGenericity ⇒ r_G ≠ s. Doing this for each old real shows r_G is new.";
    return '';
  }
  function pos(level, idx){
    const margin = 30;
    const y = 36 + level*70;

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';

    for(let L=0; L<levels.length-1; L++){

      }

        const fill = isDense ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--panel2)';

        svg.appendChild(t);

    const lg = SVG('g',{transform:'translate(440, 14)'});
    lg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:0,cy:0,r:7,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'var(--yellow)'}));
    const lt1 = SVG('text',{x:12,y:4,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});

    const lt2 = SVG('text',{x:12,y:22,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});

  buttons.forEach(b => b.addEventListener('click', () => {
    buttons.forEach(x => x.classList.remove('active'));

</script>
</section>

2. Generic filters and the model $M[G]$

Fix a countable transitive model $M\models$ ZFC and a poset $\mathbb P\in M$. A subset $G\subseteq\mathbb P$ is a filter if it is upward-closed ($p\in G,\ q\ge p\Rightarrow q\in G$) and downward-directed ($p,q\in G\Rightarrow\exists r\in G,\ r\le p,q$). It is $M$-generic if additionally:

\[ G\cap D \ne \emptyset \quad\text{for every dense } D\in M. \]

The countability of $M$ is essential: there are only countably many dense sets in $M$, so we can enumerate them $D_0,D_1,\dots$ and pick a descending chain $p_0\ge p_1\ge\dots$ with $p_n\in D_n$. The upward closure of this chain is the desired generic filter — and it lives outside $M$, since meeting every dense set is precisely what no $M$-element does.

Building a generic filter step by step
step ↑ to enumerate dense sets and pick a witness from each

From a generic $G$ we construct $M[G]$ by interpreting $\mathbb P$-names from $M$. A name $\dot x\in M$ is a set of pairs $(\dot y, p)$; its evaluation is $\dot x_G = \{\,\dot y_G : (\dot y, p)\in\dot x \text{ for some } p\in G\,\}$. The model is $M[G] = \{\dot x_G : \dot x\in M\}$.

Three theorems (Cohen). For any countable transitive $M\models$ ZFC, any $\mathbb P\in M$, and any $M$-generic $G\subseteq\mathbb P$:
  • Existence of $G$. $M$-generic filters always exist (in $V$, outside $M$).
  • $M\subseteq M[G]$ and $G\in M[G]$. The extension keeps everything from the ground model and exhibits $G$ as a set.
  • $M[G]\models$ ZFC. The new model satisfies all the same axioms.

So $M[G]$ is a legitimate place to do mathematics. The question — what new statements hold there — is settled by the forcing relation in the next section.

See also
  • Löwenheim–Skolem and Skolem's paradox

Used in
Forcing and independence · The forcing relation and truth lemma

  const dense = [
    {name:'D_0: bit 0 = 1',     test: s => s.length>=1 && s[0]==='1', label:'b0=1'},

    if(stage>0){

      const dt = SVG('text',{x, y:y+4,'font-size':14,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      dt.textContent = '…';
      svg.appendChild(dt);

    // commentary
    if(stage===0){
      out.textContent =
`step 0: filter is empty.  start from p_0 = ⟨⟩ (the empty condition).
press "step" to meet D_0, D_1, …, in order.

genericity DEFINITION:  G meets every D ∈ M.
since M is countable, only countably many D's exist; we enumerate them.`;
    } else if(stage<dense.length){
      out.textContent =
`step ${stage}: just met ${dense[stage-1].name}.
chain so far: p_0 ≥ p_1 ≥ … ≥ p_${stage} = "${chain[stage]}"
the upward closure of this chain is a filter.
meeting all ${dense.length} dense sets gives an M-generic filter G — and G ∉ M, since no element of M can meet every D ∈ M.`;

3. The forcing relation and truth lemma

So far the construction has been semantic: pick $G$, look at $M[G]$. The miracle is that everything true in $M[G]$ is decided by data already in $M$. The bookkeeping device is the forcing relation $p\Vdash\varphi$, defined inside $M$ by recursion on the formula $\varphi$ (which is built from $\mathbb P$-names). Read $p\Vdash\varphi$ as "every generic $G$ containing $p$ makes $\varphi$ true in $M[G]$".

Forcing relation — three properties.
  • Definability. The relation $\Vdash$ is definable in $M$. So $M$ knows which conditions force which formulas.
  • Monotonicity. If $p\Vdash\varphi$ and $q\le p$, then $q\Vdash\varphi$. Stronger conditions inherit forced statements.
  • Density. For every $\varphi$, the set $\{p : p\Vdash\varphi \text{ or } p\Vdash\neg\varphi\}$ is dense in $\mathbb P$. So every generic decides every formula.
Truth lemma. For every formula $\varphi(\dot x_1,\dots,\dot x_n)$ in the forcing language and every $M$-generic $G$: \[ M[G]\models\varphi(\dot x_{1,G},\dots,\dot x_{n,G}) \iff \exists p\in G,\ p\Vdash\varphi(\dot x_1,\dots,\dot x_n). \] Equivalently: truth in $M[G]$ is decided by the conditions that ended up in $G$.

The widget below shows a tiny forcing language with three names and four atomic formulas, and lets you flip a condition $p$ to see which atoms it forces:

Forcing relation table for a tiny example
click a condition $p$ to see what $p\Vdash$

Why "ZFC holds in $M[G]$". One proves inside $M$ that for every ZFC axiom $\sigma$, the maximal condition $\mathbb 1$ forces $\sigma$. By the truth lemma (and $\mathbb 1\in G$ for every filter), $M[G]\models\sigma$. So $M[G]\models$ ZFC for every generic $G$, regardless of which $\mathbb P$ you used.

Used in
Forcing and independence · Cohen forcing for adding reals

4. Cohen forcing for adding reals

The poset $\mathbb P=2^{<\omega}$ — the binary tree of finite strings — is Cohen forcing. A generic filter $G$ is a chain of finite strings cofinal in length, and its collapse is a single new infinite binary sequence:

\[ r_G \;=\; \bigcup_{p\in G} p \;\in\; 2^\omega. \]

The Cohen real $r_G$ is genuinely new: $r_G\notin M$. To see it, fix any $s\in (2^\omega)^M$. The set $D_s=\{\,p\in 2^{<\omega} : \exists n,\ p(n)\ne s(n)\,\}$ is in $M$ and is dense: given $p$, extend $p$ by appending the bit opposite to $s$ at the next index. Genericity of $G$ then forces $r_G\ne s$. Since this argument runs for every $s\in M$, the Cohen real avoids every old real.

Building a Cohen real bit by bit
step ↑ to extend $p$ by a uniformly random bit

Side-by-side product: forcing $\neg$CH

To add many new reals at once, take instead

\[ \mathbb P \;=\; \operatorname{Fn}(\aleph_2\times\omega,\, 2), \]

the set of finite partial functions from $\aleph_2\times\omega$ to $\{0,1\}$, ordered by extension. A condition is a finite list of commitments "at index $\alpha\in\aleph_2$ and bit position $n\in\omega$, the value is $b$". A generic $G$ defines $\aleph_2$ many distinct reals $r_\alpha\in 2^\omega$ in $M[G]$, indexed by $\alpha<\aleph_2^M$.

Cardinal preservation. $\mathbb P$ has the countable chain condition (ccc): every antichain is countable, by a $\Delta$-system argument on the finite supports. The ccc preserves cardinals and cofinalities — no $\aleph_\alpha^M$ collapses in $M[G]$. So $\aleph_2^{M[G]}=\aleph_2^M$, and the new reals stay distinct as elements of $2^\omega$ in $M[G]$.

Conclusion: $|2^\omega|^{M[G]}\ge\aleph_2^{M[G]} > \aleph_1^{M[G]}$, so $M[G]\models\neg\mathrm{CH}$. We will draw the picture in the next section.

5. Independence of the continuum hypothesis

Putting the pieces together. Let $M$ be a countable transitive model of ZFC + CH (Gödel's $L$ if you like). Let $\mathbb P=\operatorname{Fn}(\aleph_2^M\times\omega,2)$. Let $G$ be $M$-generic. Then:

stepfacttool
1$M[G]\models$ ZFCgeneric extension theorem
2$\aleph_2^{M[G]}=\aleph_2^M$ (no collapse)ccc + $\Delta$-system lemma
3$M[G]$ contains $\aleph_2^M$ pairwise distinct realsdensity: for $\alpha\ne\beta$, $D_{\alpha\beta}=\{p:p$ distinguishes $r_\alpha,r_\beta\}$ is dense
4so $|2^\omega|^{M[G]}\ge\aleph_2^{M[G]} > \aleph_1^{M[G]}$by 2 + 3
5$M[G]\models\neg\mathrm{CH}$by 4
Independence of CH. Combined with Gödel's $L\models$ ZFC + CH:
  • ZFC + CH is consistent (Gödel 1940, $L$).
  • ZFC + $\neg$CH is consistent (Cohen 1963, forcing).
So neither CH nor $\neg$CH is provable in ZFC: CH is independent of ZFC.

The aleph ladder below shows where the various consistent values of $2^{\aleph_0}$ live, with the König barrier marking the only constraint ZFC actually imposes.

Where can $2^{\aleph_0}$ land?
consistent ZFC values, with the König exclusion zone

What ZFC actually pins down. The only ZFC theorem about $2^{\aleph_0}$ is König's: $\operatorname{cf}(2^{\aleph_0}) > \omega$. So $2^{\aleph_0}\ne\aleph_\omega$, $\aleph_{\omega+\omega}$, etc. — but every cardinal of uncountable cofinality is consistent (Easton's theorem extends this to all regular cardinals, with the obvious monotonicity constraint).

6. Other independence results and Martin's axiom

Forcing did not stop with CH. The same general machine — pick a poset, force, decode the truth in the extension via $\Vdash$ — settles a long list of questions ZFC cannot.

statementstatus under ZFCwhich forcing
CH: $2^{\aleph_0}=\aleph_1$independentGödel's $L$ (CH); Cohen's product (¬CH)
SH: every Suslin tree is countableindependentSolovay–Tennenbaum iterated forcing (SH); Jensen's $\diamondsuit$ in $L$ (¬SH)
MA: Martin's axiomindependent of ZFC + ¬CHSolovay–Tennenbaum 1971: ccc iterated forcing of length $\aleph_2$
Whitehead problem: every Whitehead group is freeindependent (Shelah, 1974)under $\diamondsuit$: yes; under MA + ¬CH: no
Borel determinacy refinementrequires more than ZFC for higher pointclasseslarge cardinals + forcing (Martin, Steel, Woodin)
Indestructibility of supercompactsrelative consistency theoremLaver preparation (1978)

Martin's axiom — a parametric weakening of CH

Martin's axiom MA says: for every ccc poset $\mathbb P$ and every family $\mathcal D$ of $<2^{\aleph_0}$ dense sets, a filter $G\subseteq\mathbb P$ meeting every $D\in\mathcal D$ exists. Under CH, MA is automatic (only countable families). Under $\neg$CH, it is a substantive axiom that asserts generic-like filters exist for small problems even though no truly generic filter does.

MA + $\neg$CH is consistent (Solovay–Tennenbaum). It implies the Suslin hypothesis, that $2^{\aleph_0}$ is regular, that every Aronszajn tree is special, and many combinatorial principles. Working set theorists routinely treat MA as an "extra dial" sitting alongside ZFC.

What depends on what
click a node to see consistency relationships

The big picture. Independence is the rule, not the exception, once you leave the safe haven of arithmetic. The set-theoretic universe is not a single object — it is a multiverse, and forcing is the device that lets us hop between members. CH is the most famous member of the long list above, but everyday questions in algebraic topology (Whitehead), measure theory (cardinal invariants of the continuum), and analysis (the Suslin problem) all live there too.

7. Connections

Forcing is one of the few set-theoretic techniques that has reshaped how mathematicians think about provability — its consequences spill out of pure set theory into model theory, topology, and combinatorics.

Continuum hypothesis and beyond

Cohen's theorem closes the question Hilbert posed in 1900 — but only by saying both CH and $\neg$CH are unfalsifiable. CH inside ZFC develops the upside; model theory uses the same machinery to construct saturated structures of any cardinality.

Gödel's constructible universe $L$

Forcing extends downward via inner models: $L$ is a model of ZFC + GCH + V=L, and Cohen extensions are the orthogonal direction. First-order logic supplies the syntactic backbone every inner-model argument runs on.

Topological forcing and Boolean-valued models

A forcing poset $\mathbb{P}$ is the same data as a complete Boolean algebra of regular open sets in a topology. Topology and topos theory are the natural conceptual frames; sheaf-theoretic forcing is the modern recasting.

Combinatorics of the continuum

Independence results for cardinal invariants of the continuum (Suslin's problem, Whitehead's problem, Borel-conjecture failures) read as a forcing-driven inventory of which combinatorial questions are decided by ZFC and which are not.

Open frontiers: large-cardinal axioms (measurable, Woodin, supercompact) which provide a "natural" indestructibility theory for forcing extensions; iterated forcing and proper-forcing axioms (PFA, MM); inner-model program (Mitchell, Steel) attempting canonical models for very large cardinals.
